# **2025 – Linkedin posts**

## **Posts**

### **Mon 20 Jan 2025 – yellow jumper**

Italian clothes markets are the best.

A riot of colours and unbelievable bargains, and of good quality on top.

So when I was in Siena over Christmas, I went to the market and bought myself a bright yellow jumper (for the princely sum of 8 euros).

Ah, the sheer joy of it\!

This jumper seems to have the magic power of making other people happy, too. People always make a positive comment about it.

It’s the perfect shade of yellow: just like a sunflower. Looks great with black, and I feel like a bee\!

In fact, yellow is a positive colour in many cultures. In Africa it's often reserved to aristocrats; in Japan it represents bravery; in Thailand it's the colour of luck; in imperial China, a particular shade was reserved for emperors.

Blog posts on colour psychology usually say that yellow is the happiest colour on the spectrum, evoking sunshine, hope, laughter, warmth, and energy.

But yellow is not always positive.

In English, cowards have a 'yellow streak'; in Germany, jealousy is yellow; in medieval France, criminals' doors were painted yellow; in the 1800s, books with yellow covers were sensationalist at best, and scandalous at worst. A colder yellow with a tinge of green is the colour of jaundice and ill health. And then there’s Ryanair’s acid yellow, of course: the perfect shade of what I call “vile yellow”.

Plus: yellow is one of the easiest colours to get wrong online.

Why?

– Yellow on a screen can bring eye strain and fatigue: our long and medium cones are highly sensitive to its wavelength.

– Yellow colours on a digital platform often have 100% saturation and brightness: this can almost physically hurt sensitive eyes.

– Yellow text on a white background is too low contrast to be legible; reversing background and foreground won’t help.

– Yellow is not easy to tweak for contrast: darkening it makes it brown which definitely doesn’t provide the same “happy yellow” effect now, does it.

So, should you use yellow online, at all?

… it depends.

You can definitely use it if:

– You use it on a limited surface area and not the whole background

– You pair it correctly

– You avoid the "vile yellow" range (

[hashtag](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/?keywords=ffff00&highlightedUpdateUrns=urn%3Ali%3Aactivity%3A7287027825528725506)

[\#FFFF00](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/?keywords=ffff00&highlightedUpdateUrns=urn%3Ali%3Aactivity%3A7287027825528725506) and yellows in its vicinities.

– You prefer a slightly more golden hue (

[hashtag](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/?keywords=ffd000&highlightedUpdateUrns=urn%3Ali%3Aactivity%3A7287027825528725506)

[\#FFD000](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/?keywords=ffd000&highlightedUpdateUrns=urn%3Ali%3Aactivity%3A7287027825528725506) and yellows in its vicinities)

– You take saturation and brightness down a notch (even down to 95% would help)

– You make sure you use a contrast checking tool such as Colour Contrast Checker

– You test with low vision people/ people with eye sensitivities/ people with neurological conditions.

I have palinopsia, for instance, and vile yellow properly hurts my eyes.

Why am I telling you all this?

Because I am hard at work on the Get It Seen Masterclass, happening on 25th Feb 2025\!

It will be a class on accessibility, yes: but we'll talk about colour – a lot.

Want to start learning right now? Download my Colour Cheat Sheet:

go to piccianeri dot com/ccs

Image description: two bright yellow sunflowers against a deep blue sky.

### **Wed 22 Jan 2025 – open captions**

(I deleted it, it needs new image)

You know what’s as fun as a whisky hangover?

Being forced to watch videos with fancy flashing captions in all caps, thick fonts and other cool effects.

Yep: the burnt-in captions on your videos are literally making billions of people sick.

And I know that's not what you want\!

But every time you post that kind of caption, someone among your viewers will be closing the window in haste.

I know\! It seems wild, especially now that @LinkedIn is pushing video so much. And yet, true.

For those of us with a vestibular disorder – affecting inner ear sensory mechanisms – being forced to watch those captions without any agency to turn them off is as fun as an accidental overdose of whisky (if you know, you know\! 🤢).

Wondering how I can boldly claim it’s billions of us? 

Here are a few figures:

– 35% of US adults age 40+ (ca 69 million) have evidence of vestibular dysfunction

– 4% (ca 8 million) US adults report chronic balance issues.  

– 1.1% report chronic dizziness.  

– 80% age 65+ experience dizziness.

Beyond those of us made dizzy, there are many other categories negatively affected by this type of caption:

– 2.2 billion with a vision disability

– 75 million on the autistic spectrum

– 50 million with epilepsy

– 78 million with dyslexia

– 200 million with a cognitive impairment

And crucially:

– 1.5 billion (not a typo) people with some form of hearing loss.

I know that you think you're helping when you add your fancy captions\! But I'm sorry, you're probably not.

Please stop doing this:

– Open captions (can’t be turned off)

– One word at a time

– All in caps

– Alternating cases

– Unpredictable layouts

– Flashing effects & whirlwind animations

– Fancy typeface

– Fancy colours

– Against a busy, colourful background

– In the middle, top, or other awkward placement

Do this instead:

– Closed captions (can be turned off)

– Short sentence at a time (ca. 40 characters on max 2 lines)

– Meaningful line breaks

– Sentence case

– Familiar layout

– NO flashing effects or animations EVER

– Simple sans serif font

– Almost-white or sensible colour type

– Against 80% opacity almost-black (with exceptions)

– At the bottom

Bad captions are almost worse than no captions.

Use your creativity to make your content engaging and unforgettable, as well as easy for everyone to understand – and buy from you.

Accessibility improves conversions\! Always.

Captions aid cognition, help attention span, and help learn new languages.

Do you also enable closed captions when you watch a video?

Image description: Still from a video interview by influencer Steven Bartlett to Dr Daniel Lieberman, a white man with glasses, white hair and beard. The caption says "the vast majority of the evidence suggests that", & sits on top of Dr Lieberman's shirt. It alternates caps, lowercase, italics, in white bold type with black drop-shadow, on 5 separate lines, each with a different anchor point.

\#DontBeADick \#a11y \#accessibility

Posted this instead, repurposed from the past: 

A few years ago, I stopped being a dick online and embraced accessibility.

And no:

– Accessibility doesn’t make the web “boring”

– Accessibility doesn’t mean “not creative”

– Accessibility doesn’t bring limitations

– Accessibility is the main ingredient of good UX

When you embrace accessibility, your world opens up hugely.

Take for instance non-descriptive links. 

You know, like the bog-standard “Read more” in most article archive pages.

This seemingly innocuous invite is a frustrating mystery for those using assistive tech. (And yes, you, too, have visitors that use screen readers or other assistive technologies).

People who can’t scan a web page or an app by reading it, can decide to use links (or headings) to find out what’s in it.

(This includes loads of people who are not permanently disabled, by the way: a lot more people than you think use screen readers, or voice activated tech, or navigate a page with tabs.)

When your links are:

– “Click here”

– “Read more”

– “Go to this page”

Et similia, without any indication of content or context, many people (approx 25% of your audience) will have no idea where that link will end up.

I’m not going to “Click here”, if I don’t know where the click will take me.

In the same vein, when you have dozens of “Read more” on an archive page full of articles, you can imagine the frustration they might cause.

Why take the time to write a piece and link to it, if you don’t make sure people will know what it’s about?

I certainly won’t “Go to the page”, either, if I have no idea why I should bother.

Do this instead:

– Make links more descriptive: “Explore \[company name\]’s web design services”

– Make the article title in the archive a clickable link, and get rid of “Read more” altogether

– Make it clear within the text what the contents of the page will be, and link to that instead: “You can buy \[the product\] here”.

Another positive side-effect? Of course, much better SEO juice.

Stop being a dick online. Embrace accessibility, and just like I did, you also will:

– Expand your audience massively

– Avoid fines and lawsuits 

– Earn masses of good karma

– Discover new professional opportunities you never thought existed.

Here's another post I wrote, with more tips on how to write descriptive links: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri\_accessibility-a11y-dontbeadick-activity-7253056305970483202-Vvwe?utm\_source=share\&utm\_medium=member\_desktop

\#DontBeADick \#AccessibilityMatters \#InclusiveDesign \#UXForAll \#EthicalUX \#a11y \#accessibility

### **Friday 24 Jan – captions (with new image)**

This LinkedIn trend is making me sick.

@LinkedIn wants all of us to create videos. And they're aggressively promoting it, pushing down anything that isn't moving image.

What I want you and LinkedIn to know is this:

This LinkedIn trend is literally making me sick.

Hang on a minute\! Surely that must be an exaggeration?

Nope. It's not.

It's not the videos per se, either (although it can be, when they sport sudden zooms, flashing images, or intentional camera shake).

Mostly, it's those blessed open captions.

The screenshot attached to this post is from my video feed on mobile. In the video on the left, typefaces appear on screen at vertiginous speed, and at different sizes, colours, weights, styles, and placements. Not to mention the rest of the visual clutter on the frame.

(link to video in comments)

This type of captions make everything worse – not better.

That kind of caption will make someone among your viewers close the window in haste – if it's me, off to puke quietly in a corner.

For those of us with a vestibular disorder – affecting inner ear sensory mechanisms – being forced to watch a video without agency to turn captions off is as fun as an accidental overdose of whisky (if you know, you know 🤢).

If you think I'm exaggerating, here are a few figures:

– 35% of US adults age 40+ (ca 69 million) have evidence of vestibular dysfunction

– 4% (ca 8 million) US adults report chronic balance issues  

– 1.1% report chronic dizziness

– 80% age 65+ experience dizziness

Beyond those of us made dizzy, there are plenty other categories negatively affected by the wild captions trend:

– 2.2 billion with a vision disability

– 75 million on the autistic spectrum

– 50 million with epilepsy

– 78 million with dyslexia

– 200 million with a cognitive impairment

And crucially:

– 1.5 billion (not a typo) people with some form of hearing loss.

Most deaf/ hard of hearing people report that the addition of fancy captions makes it virtually impossible for them to understand a video. So the category that would benefit the most from captions, is actively excluded by the wrong captions.

I know that you think you're helping when you add fancy captions\! But I'm sorry, you're probably not.

Please, I beg you – stop doing this:

– Open captions (burnt in, can’t be turned off)

– One word at a time

– All in caps

– Alternating cases

– Unpredictable layouts

– Flashing effects & whirlwind animations

– Fancy typeface

– Fancy colours

– Against a busy, colourful background

– In the middle, top, or other awkward placement

Do this instead:

– Closed captions (can be turned off)

– 1 Short sentence at a time (ca. 40 characters on max 2 lines)

– Meaningful line breaks

– Sentence case

– Familiar layout

– NO flashing effects or animations EVER

– Simple sans serif font

– Almost-white or sensible colour type

– Against 80% opacity almost-black (with exceptions)

– At the bottom

Please, always remember: bad captions are worse than no captions.

Thank you.

### **Monday 27 Jan 2025 – Sharing Guidesight’s launch on Product Hunt**

Proximity blindness is A THING\!

What thing?

That thing that makes you cross your eyes when you've been working on a project for too long.

That thing that makes you overlook the most obvious blunders and bloopers in your own copy that you've been looking at for days.

That thing that makes it impossible for you to work out why your website isn't converting, after you worked so hard at it\!

That's why UX and UI reviews are SO helpful.

Someone else's fresh pair of very trained, experienced eyes on your product/ website to point out what works, what doesn't, what is accessible, what isn't, what could be better, what is already the best.

Resulting in a series of immediately actionable tips that can bring quick and very welcome wins.

Any client I've ever done an audit for, has deemed it transformational.

I am very thorough: I look at the user journey and take deep dives wherever that takes me, all the while making accessibility and usability notes.

All of the above takes a LONG time to order and compile manually – believe me. When in fact, the issues you look at can be categorised and are always repeated.

Enter @Guidesight by the wonderful @Madalin Chirila. An audit tool that streamlines the process, making it so much easier for the client to parse through the notes. And making my work look so professional and sleek – while saving me heaps of time. An absolute game changer. I still integrate with personalised videos, but Guidesight is now the backbone of my audits, and I am so grateful for it.

So I am delighted that it is launching on @Product Hunt today\! Mark my words: this is a brilliant tool\! Go vote for it, NOW\!\!\!

[https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri\_ux-userexperience-cro-activity-7289580525520842752-7UpH?utm\_source=share\&utm\_medium=member\_desktop](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri_ux-userexperience-cro-activity-7289580525520842752-7UpH?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop)

### **Tue 28 Jan 2025 – recycling centred text**

I want to live in a world where...

... Nobody ever centres their text.

Bet you thought I'd stop the war.

\[I do wish I could\]

Today, I just don't want you to centre your text. Ever.

Better said: you can centre text on a wedding invitation, or on a tombstone.

Any other time you feel tempted, ask yourself:

"Is this a wedding invitation?"

"Is this a tombstone?"

If the answer is "no" to either question, then:

❌ Do not centre your text.

– Centred text is basically not aligned: a usability & accessibility issue.

– Every time a line ends there's a lost moment as the reader spends precious time locating the beginning of the next.

– With no clear edge to focus on, you're increasing the cognitive load.

– And making the visitor work harder to focus on where to go next.

– Counter-productive for everyone: the humans visiting, & the site owner, hoping to make a conversion.

– People with vision issues have an even harder time.

– So do people with neuro-atypical issues such as dyslexia and/ or autism.

– People like me who see shapes instead of text: sometimes it's a UFO, at other times a vase,or a hat...

✅ Do this instead:

– For a Western language, align left. That's where your visitors' eye will fall first anyway: make use of that.

– Don't be tempted to justify: it causes unsightly gaps & cognitive delays.

– Think of the content structure: is this the title of the page, and therefore an h1? Using correct HTML hierarchy will help screen readers scan your page & make sense of the content. This is a great SEO tip by the way, on top of accessibility.

– Enhance usability by employing text size and weight to create visual hierarchy and lead the eye of your visitors to where you want it. Use the power of design\!

– Use colour sparingly and never to convey any crucial meaning or differentiation.

– Use a contrast checker, e.g. https://colorcontrast dot app – always.

Follow this advice, and you'll automatically convert more.

There is no way I can back this wild claim, but I *know* it's true.

✅ Recapping:

– Left-align – & use alignment in the layout.

– Ragged lines, never justified.

– Correct HTML structure for SEO & accessibility.

– Visual hierarchy for better usability.

– Always use a contrast checker.

Image description: Slide 1 shows the “Piccia Neri” logo on the top left. In the centre of the slide is a block of white text centred on a black background with each line beginning randomly. It says:"“Centred alignment is basically lack of alignment. It looks messy and untidy. The eye has nowhere to anchor.

The lines are ragged in an unpredictable way. It is disorienting and hard to read for neuro-divergent people, those with low vision issues, and old type nerds like me.” Below the text block is an emoji face crying rivers. Slide 2 shows is the same block of text in white on black, aligned to the left. Below the text block is a emoji face with a wide smile.

hashtag\#a11y hashtag\#accessibility hashtag\#typography

### **Thursday 30 Jan – NOT forever young you ageist cunts.** 

One day.

One day, it will happen to you, too.

You will be over 50\.

And on that day, you, too, will hate the forms that end their age range question with 50+.

(If you are already over 50, you know exactly what I mean.)

I can assure you, 50 is not 60 is not 70\.

I am 57 (how is that even possible, and yet, here we are) and I am categorically not the same as I was when merely a sprightly young 50\.

And I know that when I am 67, I will look back nostalgically at the me of today and I will see a spring chicken in comparison.

Design for your future self: instead of shoving all of us 50+ in the same category, or patronising them with cutesy "Forever young" options, provide us with the same nuance everybody else gets.

(this is excellent accessible design advice in general, by the way)

Plus, the way things are going, we’ll all have to carry on working until we drop dead, so this will matter to you too one day.

I am JOKING I wish everyone an early retirement if that’s what you want.

But even so: there are so many of us in the workplace that are over 50, over 60, over 65 or even 75\. And we are not all the same.

Ageism is a thing – keep it off your forms.

Thank you.

p.s. That question is from the sign-up flow to Smashing Magazine's event "Smashing Meets Accessibility" which is free and you should definitely attend, whatever your age.

Image description: Multiple choice question asking: "What's your age?"; with checkbox answers:

\< 20

20-29 yrs

30-39 yrs

40-49 yrs

\> 50

Forever young

\#ageism \#inclusion \#inclusiveDesign

### **Tuesday 4 Feb 2025 – Captions and meetup talk**

What you see:

"A fancy new captions tool\! Don't you love AI? This looks great\! I'll get noticed\!"

What you don’t see:

All the people that the fancy new captions alienate from your posts, forever\!

A while ago, a group of people in a membership I used to be in got very excited about a captioning tool.

So I thought “oh great\! People who care about captions”.

But then I went to watch the Instagram reel they were talking about. The image accompanying this post shows a screenshot I took.

A young man with a beard is reciting “A poem about homes and being in them”. He’s sitting in a living room and holding a mug. A caption in all caps, centred text and handwritten font is floating on his face, almost covering his mouth. The words “Then I’d like the word imperfections to come before” are centred vertically and horizontally on top of the image.

A sentence that, taken out of context, is incomprehensible. 

But the worst is that even when in context I – and many others with me – cannot see it, let alone understand its meaning.

Why? Let me tell you all the reasons:

1. A handwritten font is much harder to read.  
2. All caps slow down speed of reading.  
3. All caps create legibility issues. For instance, people with dyslexia find it very difficult to tell all caps words apart.  
4. On top of that, each word appearing one by one and fading super quickly makes it even harder to comprehend the whole sentence.  
5. The words in a thin, small typeface, floating on top of a busy background are very hard to see.  
6. They’re very close to the mouth which will cause problems for those relying on lip-reading.  
7. The speed of the animation causes motion sickness for people with vestibular disorders (i.e. inner ear issues. Hello\! I’m one of those).

In a nutshell: this fancy way of captioning is an accessibility disaster.

I watched this video and just suffered through it, without understanding anything or being able to properly read any of it.

How to sort this:

1. Don't use all caps for captions. There's no sorting this one.  
2. Ideally, you won't use a handwritten typeface, either.  
3. But if you have to (although you really don't have to, and you shouldn't), make it way bigger.  
4. And give it an 80-90% opacity background, with enough contrast with the typeface colour.  
5. Don't place it so close to someone's mouth.  
6. Don't place it in the middle of the video, or on a busy background.  
7. Don't use fancy animations. There's no sorting this one, either.

You are literally making me sick.

If a low-vision person came to your brick and mortar shop, you wouldn't slam the door in their face. Would you?

So, please don't do it in your digital venues\!

Want to learn more about captions? Join us at the @Equalize Digital meetup on Thursday 6 March 2025 at 5pm CET\! Link to join in comments\!

And thank you @Amber Hinds for having me\!

For the series: \#DontBeADick \#BeAccessible

\#accessibility \#AccessibilityMatters \#a11y

### **Thursday 6 Feb – Dear designers recycled**

Dear designers: you're worth way more than you think.

Here's your overdue reminder.

The problem with the web used to be:

Suddenly everyone thought they could be a designer.

(not sure why, given drag and drop design tools have existed for ages, but nobody thought they'd design their own gatefold brochure or exhibition catalogue)

The problem with the web now is:

Everyone still thinks they could be a designer, plus: suddenly everyone thinks AI can substitute a designer.

It breaks my heart when I see and hear designers feeling that they've become irrelevant. But to me, the opposite is true.

Every time I carry out an audit, watch a webinar, or simply use the web, I see s\*\*t designs and dickish behaviour that show zero consideration to the people using the product, or watching the presentation. Clearly, not designed by a trained designer.

– Low contrast

– Wonky layouts

– Dead-end user journeys

– Visual clutter

– Reckless animations

– Terrible typography \> inaccessible, plus cognitive overload

– Terrible typesetting \> inaccessible, plus cognitive overload

– Fancy captions that are impossible to watch

The result?

Accessibility issues galore.

Important reminder: best-practice design is natively accessible.

This is how a designer would do it:

– Correct contrast

– Tight, grid-based layouts

– Well thought-out user journeys that convert

– Plenty of breathing space, no visual fluff

– Animations have a reason to exist and they're implemented responsibly

– Flawless typography \> accessible, and aiding cognition

– Flawless type layout \> accessible, and aiding cognition

– Sensible captions that serve the purpose

The result?

Natively accessible products.

(there's more to it, I know, but this is the best start).

So when in doubt: ask a designer. They do know better than you – believe me.

And they will make you money – as well as save you money in the long run.

[\#a11y](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7294667821517541377/#) [\#AccessibleDesign](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7294667821517541377/#) [\#AccessibilityMatters](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7294667821517541377/#)

[\#DontBeADick](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7294667821517541377/#)

### **Monday 10 Feb 2025 – train your staff on a11y**

[https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri\_want-to-save-money-and-make-money-on-activity-7294667821517541377-2-by?utm\_source=share\&utm\_medium=member\_desktop](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri_want-to-save-money-and-make-money-on-activity-7294667821517541377-2-by?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop)

Want to save money – and make money – on accessibility?

Train your staff.

I say this to every client, and I’ll keep saying it because it’s true: if you invest in accessibility training for your team now, you’ll spend a lot less fixing problems later.

And you will also open up your audience to a lot more people.

Here’s what happens when your team doesn’t know accessibility:

– They create inaccessible content

– You have to rely on expensive external help

– You waste time retro-fitting instead of doing it right the first time

– You risk legal trouble (trust me on this one – the European Accessibility Act will open up the floor to this)

– You shut out 25% of your audience – yes, that’s the percentage of people who have a disability at any given time

Here’s what happens when your team does know accessibility:

– They create accessible content from the start

– You don’t have to call in expensive external help as often

– You reach a bigger audience and make more money

– You avoid legal headaches

– You build a better, more inclusive brand

– You turn accessibility into an opportunity – instead of a limitation

The choice is yours. But if you’re going to wait until you get sued, lose customers, or have to rebuild everything from scratch… don’t say I didn’t warn you.

2 things you can do now:

– Download my handy guide to the European Accessibility Act, and check whether it applies to you or not: got to piccianeri dot com/eaa/ (proper link in comments)

– Send me a message to chat about how I can help you turn accessibility into an opportunity, by training your teams.

Happy Monday, by the way\!

And here's a fluffy white cat in a basket for the algo, and for your delectation. He's seen from above and is looking up at the camera with huge blue eyes. There's a hint of orange in the light markings on his face, and on his coat. It's clear from the floof volume that he'd smell of talcum powder, should you bury your face in that gorgeous softness. 

### **Friday 14 Feb 2025 – the problem with accessibility \+ poll**

You know what the problem with accessibility truly is?

It’s very simple: accessibility is considered really boring at best, and a total pain in the arse at worst.

Boring (especially for designers, or design-adjacent peeps):

– The old chestnut that “it stunts creativity”: accessibility can't be beautiful, and we can’t have stuff flying around the screen willy-nilly no more, or use “bold” colour combos that might hurt someone’s eyes, or incomprehensible typography.

Pain in the arse (especially for business owners, or CEOs):

– Have to spend money on remediating, rebuilding and training, in constant fear of legal retaliation.

Now, I won’t lie to you. There have been moments when having to make something accessible has been – let’s say, complicated. Mostly, the “complication” was created by the client’s reluctance to accept that a certain accessibility feature was essential.

But I’d be lying if I didn’t confess that I’ve had to adapt the way I think about design.

I am, after all, a recovering creative – and I can assure you that past me, the one designing film posters, for instance, didn’t give visual accessibility a second thought – ever.

First item on the list was: get bums on cinema seats; second was: do it in the coolest, most visually appealing, most creative, cleverest way possible.

But I tell you what the crucial ingredient to get to the best solution always was and always is, whatever you are designing:

A good brief.

You know the old adage “garbage in, garbage out”, I’m sure. Any successful design comes from a good brief.

Plus, creating without a strict, clear brief is the hardest thing of all: “free rein”, i.e. a lack of direction, often leads to despair. Too many options \= no option.

So that’s how I’d love for you, my LinkedIn peeps, whether you are designers, design-adjacent, or in fact, doing anything at all on the web, to consider accessibility:

As an excellent brief.

Can accessibility double up as a creative brief?

There is no doubt for me. Yes. Yes it can.

In the new year I will embark on a series of big educational projects for global brands as well as conferences – and that’s exactly the angle I will work from.

The aim is to make sure I get teams on board with accessibility out of their own conviction – not just because legal said they must.

So, my question to you today is:

Can accessibility double up as a creative brief?

Or do you believe, deep down, that it's just a pain in the arse?

### **Mon 3 March 2025 – recycled**

I picked up a car at the airport in Amman in the dead of night, and drove all over Jordan by myself for a whole month…

… thanks to digital maps.

I’ve come to rely on digital maps to find my way around.

But did you know that maps can make some people lose their way, instead of finding it?

I gave an accessibility workshop to a company that creates a lot of data visualisations. This is what I found:

1. There is no such thing as a fully accessible map (yet).

I am lucky to be able to use digital maps when I need them.

2. There is no better way to speed up accessibility efforts than seeing a disabled person struggle with, and ultimately be defeated by, something your company built.

(I asked @Lazar Bulatovic to interact with a map this company built, and he found it impossible.)

 3\. There are a lot of alternatives to colour in data visualisation (thank you, @Tobias Christian Jensen)

    

 4\. Alt text writing is an undervalued skill that should be learnt by anyone working on the web.

    

 5\. It is so easy to dismiss an image as decorative and not worthy of a description, just because you can see it.

    

 6\. There is such a thing as too clever. When a chart or a map are too complicated, you need to split them up.

    

 7\. The alt text to a chart or a map will help anyone understand the data better, regardless of ability, and will lessen the cognitive load. Take the time to describe your data visualisations, and put the description in the front end.

    

 8\. Once again: the majority of accessibility issues stem from design. Bad design choices are inaccessible.

    

 9\. They also stem from content. Badly organised and badly written content is inaccessible.

    

10. Of course, accessible code matters, too. But developers code the content and design they're given, so start from there.

    

11. We make a lot of assumptions when we know our content too well. Pretend you know nothing, and write your content from that point.

    

12. Accessibility is a mindset shift. It takes time and patience to get a whole company to make that shift.  
13. **Prevention is much better than cure**: teach yourself and your staff to create accessible content from the start, and you’ll see a massive change – plus, you’ll save a lot of time, money and future headaches.

    

Want to prevent, rather than cure?

I'm running my Get It Seen Masterclass again this Wednesday\!

Listen what @Luminita (Lumi) Ciobanu had to say about it:

“I attended Piccia's Get It Seen Masterclass on colour and contrast, and I found it very useful. Piccia doesn’t just share knowledge—she brings passion to every step of the design process. I recommend this masterclass: it’s a truly valuable investment\!”

And it's only €97\!

Find the link in the comments\!

Image description: A road in the desert in Jordan, stretching out to the horizon, with no living creature or vehicles around. On the right, a camel crossing road sign stands against the blue sky. © Piccia Neri.

### **Tuesday 4 March – Pink/grey trainer**

Mostly, I don't care whether I'm right or wrong.

For instance, look at the training shoe in this post's photo image. To me, that's unequivocally a pink trainer. With a blue-green tinge, for sure: but pink, without a doubt.

Show this image in a crowded conference room, and these are the answers you'll get:

it's grey

it's green

it's white

it's some other colour entirely

a minority say it's pink.

Who is right? Or does it even matter?

I often boast that I usually get it right when interpreting a colour image, because I am also a photographer/ image maker and I've played with colour all my life. And luckily, we have tools to measure digital colour.

Keen to prove my visual interpretation is correct, off I go, to check the colour of the trainer in an image manipulation program.

And – shock, horror – look at the colour picker in the second image attached to this post:

The digital image of this trainer is grey. Specifically, quite an uniform \#999f9f.

I am proved wrong my friends\! And I stand abundantly corrected.

Why do this variations in chromatic interpretation happen?

Well, you could say that the phenomenon is down to the vastly differing types of displays, environmental circumstances, and personal physiology.

But the display and circumstances don't really count when we're all in the same room, do they.

So, it's very much down to the physiology of each individual, and perhaps even other biases.

This kind of illusion, of improper colour identification, is known as a chromatic bias, and it's due to chromatic adaptation: our visual system processes colour appearance making adjustments based on light, and other circumstances.

And it appears that we all process differently\!

Isn't it fascinating?

And how do we work out how to overcome, or even harness all this beautiful chaos of unpredictable personal reactions, when we design for digital environments?

Here's the answer, my friends.

Join my Get It Seen masterclass this Wed 5 March, at 4pm CET\!

(Link in comments)

We'll spend about 3 hours working out how to make colour work effectively and accessibly with all other elements on a page, in accordance with WCAG requirements.

Why would you need it?

All the smart kids know this by now: accessible design is a competitive advantage. Especially when you think that around 67% of accessibility issues still stem from design – and the overwhelming majority of websites, even the top ones, are still getting contrast and colour all wrong.

Perhaps even more importantly: this is accessibility advice specifically conceived to clarify design issues.

So if trying to interpret the WCAG success criteria makes you come out in hives – but you do know that you must tackle that monster sooner or later – this masterclass is most definitely for you.

Last but by no means least – what colour is the trainer for you?

Very keen to know…

### **Tuesday 18 March – CF Hackathon winners**

[https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri\_cfhack-cfhack2025-cloudfest-activity-7307762363598868480-Z7cn?utm\_source=share\&utm\_medium=member\_desktop\&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri_cfhack-cfhack2025-cloudfest-activity-7307762363598868480-Z7cn?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU)

I loathe fun fairs. I have a deep hatred of roller coasters. I despise aqua parks.

So, what am I doing in an amusement park in the middle of nowhere?

Answer: the [CloudFest](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7307762363598868480/#) Hackathon of course.

In short: around 110 people meet up 3 days before the huge Cloudfest conference, somewhere in southern Germany (don't ask me about the journey here... the answer would not be safe for work or LinkedIn).

Each participant decides which one out of 10 pro-bono open source software projects they want to join, and spends 3 days contributing to it. At the end of the 3 days, each team present the results of their work, and a jury picks the winner.

I'd been meaning to join for years. This year I finally managed – thanks to the wonderful [Ninja Forms](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7307762363598868480/#) & [Quay Morgan](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7307762363598868480/#).

The choice of project was very easy: the [Visua11y \- Accessible Infographics](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7307762363598868480/#) team, working on a plugin to make infographics accessible.

Particularly apposite: I've been working with a client that relies on infographics a lot, so I'm au fait about what an infographic needs to be truly accessible.

Reader, I backed the right horse: the awesome Accessible Infographics team, led by the formidable [Anne-Mieke Bovelett](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7307762363598868480/#), won the overall prize for best project\!

The developers who hacked away at it for the entire weekend, did seem to be a bit magic. I was bowled away by the speed at which they created an MVP (Minimum Viable Product) that can already very effectively read an inaccessible graph, and churn out an accessible description for it.

My own contribution? Education, documentation, and an example: I took an inaccessible, incomprehensible horizontal bar chart, and turned it into an understandable pie chart, documenting the process step by step – soon to be a blog post for you to learn from.

So: well done me, for picking the winning team\! And huge congratulations to Anne-Mieke for putting the project together, and to everyone on the team for their incredible work. Usually, in 3 days you can expect to conceptualise an idea and get the project started: it is very unusual to end the 3 days with an actual piece of software that can be used.

Another cherry on the cake was: meeting in real life a recent new student of mine, [Luminita (Lumi) Ciobanu](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7307762363598868480/#), who was on the same team\! It was great to work with you Lumi\!

The other geniuses on the team:

[Nina Jameson](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7307762363598868480/#)

[Tobias Roppelt](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7307762363598868480/#)

[Jakob Trost](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7307762363598868480/#)

[Farhan Ahmed](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7307762363598868480/#)

[Zeshan Ahmed](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7307762363598868480/#)

Lorenz Sigrist

[Elisa Sophia Foltyn](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7307762363598868480/#)

[Tatiana Valachi](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7307762363598868480/#)

[Mike Demopoulos](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7307762363598868480/#)

[Beatriz González Mellídez, CPWA, ADS, CPUX-UR](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7307762363598868480/#)

Bernhard Kau

[Aaron Kessler](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7307762363598868480/#)

[Mario Wolf](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7307762363598868480/#)

With special thanks to the amazing [Carole Olinger](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7307762363598868480/#).

Image description: Anne-Mieke Bovelett and Tobias Roppelt on stage at CloudFest Hackathon 2025\. Anne, a white woman in her 50s wearing glasses, is talking. Behind her, a slide says: "Millions of people are unable to process important information on the web. We have the power to change that. Money • Time • Love"

**\#CFHack** **\#CFHack2025** **\#CloudFest** **\#visua11y** **\#a11y** **\#OpenSource**

### **Thursday 20 March 2025 – Poll on how people prefer to learn**

[https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri\_lessons-i-am-learning-while-creating-an-accessibility-activity-7308497479564496900-K2jQ?utm\_source=share\&utm\_medium=member\_desktop\&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri_lessons-i-am-learning-while-creating-an-accessibility-activity-7308497479564496900-K2jQ?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU)

Lessons I am learning while creating an accessibility education program for a massive global brand: 

1\. Patience is your friend. It will take much longer than you think. 

2\. Remove assumptions. Accessibility awareness is always way lower than you may naively think. 

3\. Test your beliefs. Workshops are the best way to teach by doing.

4\. Until they are not. It can be very hard to get any number of very busy people in the same room for even one hour, let alone 3\. 

5\. Check your bias. Research says that pre-recorded courses don't usually get completed: but when the lessons are bite-sized, and focused on solving one specific problem, they can be the right answer to problem 4 above.

I was really surprised by points 4 and 5\. I never thought I'd find myself proposing a pre-recorded course as an alternative to a live workshop. 

So I'd really love it if you could tell me what you think – how do you like to learn? 

I'd always prefer a workshop, but I can totally see how putting aside a big chunk of time can be almost impossible for some people.

What's your preference when you learn?

### **Tue 25 March 2025 – Infographics**

[https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri\_i-have-a-confession-to-make-im-crap-at-activity-7310231297895862272-iYFv?utm\_source=share\&utm\_medium=member\_desktop\&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri_i-have-a-confession-to-make-im-crap-at-activity-7310231297895862272-iYFv?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU)

I have a confession to make:

I'm crap at data and numbers.

While I don't think I have dyscalculia, I'm definitely mathematically challenged, and  have been since as long as I can remember.

Precisely for this reason I'm in a good position to work with data visualisations: if I can understand it, then anyone can\! I work hard at simplifying the data, so others don't have to.

Data visualisations need to tell a story: and quite often, they fail.

Take the bar chart in the first image, for example.

It's a horizontal bar chart illustrating the annual household income percentages in a geographical area, ranging from less than $20k a year (16%) to more than $200k a year (27%). The data set is pretty simple: and yet, when I look at this chart, I struggle to work out which story it's trying to tell me.

The fact that all the numerical data is on the same line confuses me; and the fact that each bar is a percentage, but I have no idea of what the whole that each percentage is part of looks like, confuses me even more. I find it hard to form a mental picture of the income levels: which means this chart has failed.

That's when I thought: this bar chart should really be a pie chart.

When should you use a pie chart? I’d say that when the total is 100%, as is the case here, that’s a textbook example of a typical use case of a pie chart.

Yes: pie charts work well when showing proportions or percentages that add up to a whole. They are useful when you need to highlight the dominant share of a dataset or compare a small number of categories.

Now when I look at the pie (see second image) and the slices each income bracket gets, the story the data tells us becomes much clearer. Even I can understand it\! And weave a significant narrative out of it (in this case: half the people in this area are rich, and a significant portion are very poor, with a big gap between them).

And as for the use of visual cues: you don't need colour to differentiate. Nope, you don't. You can use colour, if you do it properly: but you don't need it.

Why does this matter?

Because infographics help e-commerce sites sell stuff\! And they also help institutions get grants and funding.

But you can’t publish a product comparison table, and expect to make money out of your product if 25% of the population – as well as search engines – can’t see it properly, or even at all.

And you can't expect to get vital funding, if your chart makes it so hard to visualise the situation you need funding for.

What about you?

Do you use charts in your work? Do you have clients that rely heavily on infographics?

If you do, watch out for the release of Visua11y \- Accessible Infographics: a game-changing plugin to help you make accessible existing infographics, without having to start from scratch.

But you know what's also great? Getting infographics right from the beginning\!

### **Fri 28 March 2025 – Hatch** 

https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri\_you-know-what-is-perceived-by-most-people-activity-7311407168719654913-LDbM?utm\_source=share\&utm\_medium=member\_desktop\&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU

You know what is perceived by most people as a pain in the arse?

(You've probably heard me say this before).

Yes. Accessibility.

And you know which category can perceive accessibility as a personal affront to their right of expression?

Designers.

(Not all designers).

But yes: designers. More specifically: creative designers.

(In the interest of full disclosure, I am a recovering creative designer).

Creative designers often perceive accessibility as a massive spanner in the works – when they can be bothered to perceive it at all.

Bad news, kids: accessibility is here to stay. If you work in the digital realm, you cannot ignore it any further.

But here’s the good news, kids:

Accessibility is the best brief of all.

Any designer worth their chops knows that true creativity thrives within prescriptive briefs and challenges: far from being a constraint, accessibility provides an opportunity to push the boundaries of design, challenging our imagination to go beyond the obvious.

Is it easy? Nope. It ain't. Better said: not any more difficult, or any easier, than any other creative brief.

But who likes easy? The best leaps of the imagination often come from the most restrictive, most challenging briefs. The ones that force us to head for the unbeaten track.

Want to see me prove that to you?

Join me in the workshop Let Accessibility Work with your Creativity, at the legendary @Hatch Conference in Berlin next 11 Sept\! The day will be about making creativity be your inspiration, rather than your limitation.

From here till September, I will occasionally be asking you to provide examples of digital products that are creative, as well as accessible.

Spoiler alert: it is not that easy to find this kind of example.

And that's precisely why I am making this my mission:

Proving that it is possible to build websites and digital products that are beautiful, imaginative, and accessible.

To this aim: if you, your company, or your clients want to have a conversation with me on this topic, please do not hesitate to get in touch. I am looking for projects to use in the workshop in a variety of ways.

In the meantime, I'm ridiculously proud to be Hatch's first repeat speaker.

If you haven't attended Hatch before: in terms of quality, contacts, and atmosphere, it is one of the best design conferences I have ever had the privilege of speaking at. Beg, borrow, steal, to get your bosses (or your clients, or yourself) to sponsor you to attend. You won't regret it.

When you get your ticket: make sure you sign up to my workshop\!

In the meantime: do you know any website, app or product that's accessible \+ creative? If you do, please send on.

Image description: Banner announcing Piccia Neri's workshop at Hatch Conference in Berlin on 11 Sept 2025, titled "Let accessibility work with your creativity". Featuring a portrait of Piccia, a white woman in her 50s with long silver hair looking sideways at the camera with a faint smile on her face.

### **Mon 31 March – heraldry (from email to list, well done me)**

[https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri\_what-on-earth-did-the-dark-ages-ever-do-for-activity-7312446902338228224-kESl?utm\_source=share\&utm\_medium=member\_desktop\&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri_what-on-earth-did-the-dark-ages-ever-do-for-activity-7312446902338228224-kESl?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU)

What on earth did the Dark Ages ever do for accessibility?

Fun fact about me: besides a recovering creative designer, I am also an art historian by training – with 2 degrees in Italian medieval art.

This means that I know that the Dark Ages (or Middle Ages, roughly from the 6th to the beginning of the 16th century, although opinions on dates vary) were everything BUT dark.

The so-called Dark Ages were – amongst many other things – in fact kaleidoscopically imaginative, incredibly productive, often quirky, and very, very colourful.

How was colour used in the Dark Ages?

Besides the mesmerising artworks created in the period – don't get me started on those – colour was needed on the battlefield, and during tournaments.

Each faction would wear a "coat of arms", that is to say the principal part of a system of symbols used to identify an army, a city, a noble family. It was pretty essential to tell apart friend from foe; and colours played a vital part in this.

The role of coats of arms was in fact so vital, that there was a specialised profession for it: the herald.

A herald in medieval and renaissance Europe had the duty of carrying messages between the commanders of opposing armies; he also formally announced and conducted tournaments. So a herald would have to be pretty up to speed on all family derivations, and be able to recognise each symbol on sight.

Think of coat of arms as modern brand logos, if you will. (With the not-minor difference that the penalty for not recognising them might involve loss of limb or life; but I digress).

However, coats of arms didn't use all the colours of the rainbow: they used only a chosen few.

And when using colour wasn't possible, a pattern system was invented to make sure differentiation was still possible.

As you can see in the images below: a vertical lines pattern corresponded to red; a dotted pattern to yellow, or gold; a horizontal line pattern to blue.

This type of colour coding is still used today – and that's what I was using as a reference when I created the grayscale pie chart that I shared with you a few days ago.

So that's something that the Dark Ages did for us: now we can learn from heraldry how to create accessible data visualisations.

I'm preparing a workshop all on how to create accessible data visualisation, which will be full of fun bits just like this. Stay tuned for more info soon.

With huge thanks to @Tobias Christian Jensen for his wonderful workshop at @UX Copenhagen in 2023 (and to @Helle Martens for bringing such a fabulous conference into our lives)

Image 1: A coat of arms printed in black and white. Image 2: The same coat of arms printed in black and white, with added on colour on top of the respective patterns: red, yellow (or gold), and blue.

### **Tuesday 1 April – Acronyms**

[https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri\_you-know-one-thing-that-native-english-speaking-activity-7312788590499893248-U1o3?utm\_source=share\&utm\_medium=member\_desktop\&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri_you-know-one-thing-that-native-english-speaking-activity-7312788590499893248-U1o3?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU)

You know one thing that native English-speaking people seem to be very fond of – but that can be a mystery to non-natives?

Acronyms.

Yes – they are ubiquitous in English, the language I speak and write 80% of the time. And yet, they are another one of my pet peeves (I know, I do have a few).

When I first started making British friends, sometimes my new mates seemed to be using a cryptic language that was only for the initiated.

"Let me know your eeteea", one said.

What on earth is an eeteea? This was pre-internet because I am ancient, so I couldn’t ask Google. Turns out: ETA \= Estimated Time of Arrival.

I soon realised that if I wanted to sound like a native, I had to learn them all: ASAP, AWOL, FUBAR\*… or the real meaning of "See You Next Tuesday."

Post-internet the acronyms frenzy only got worse. There are SO many acronyms out in the wild now. They’re everywhere: no getting away from them.

But here’s the thing: acronyms are not accessible.

Why? Because not everyone knows what they mean, and assuming they do creates barriers. I, for one, often get my knickers in a twist with acronyms:

– I don’t remember them, unless they are pervasively obvious

– I can struggle to pronounce them: I am non-native and spelling is an acquired skill

– As they’re in all caps, the letters dance around

– And I forget them very easily.

All of the above is 100 times worse for people with dyslexia.

And there's more.

– Acronyms can confuse. I recently saw "CVD" in an article about colour contrast. Turns out it meant "Colour Vision Deficiency." But Google’s top results? "Cardiovascular Disease."

– Acronyms, when unexplained, assume knowledge. The new recruit, brilliant at their job, but with English as a second language, may not know that EOD means you need the report by the end of the day.

– Acronyms can mislead. See former British PM (Prime Minister\! See what I did there?) David Cameron's hilarious acronym blunder. He was outed as signing off his messages with “LOL” for “Lots of Love”, when it means "Laugh Out Loud”. You can blame him for many things (don't get me started), but not for that mistake.

So here’s my acronym advice:

– Spell it out: at least the first time, or in an easy to access footnote.

– Don’t assume knowledge: unless it really is extra obvious, or you know your audience very well.

– Careful with all caps: screen readers read all caps, and acronyms, letter by letter.

– Don’t be lazy: it doesn't take long to write out "Best Regards" instead of BR.

Accessibility is also about clear and inclusive communication: avoid lazy shortcuts for the initiated, that may leave other people behind.

Let’s stop assuming, and start making our writing accessible for everyone.

What about you?

Do you know of any other Cameron-level acronym blunders? Let me know\!

\*\*ASAP \= As Soon As Possible; AWOL \= Absent Without Leave, FUBAR \= Fucked UP Beyond All Repair. See comments for See You Next Tuesday

### **Thursday 3 April – Hatch 2025**

[https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri\_let-accessibility-work-with-your-creativity-activity-7313590200704638978-WrAi?utm\_source=share\&utm\_medium=member\_desktop\&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri_let-accessibility-work-with-your-creativity-activity-7313590200704638978-WrAi?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU)

I already told you:

I'm a recovering creative.

No, it doesn't mean that being a creative is an addiction you should overcome.

It means that I've stopped serving my own ego when using my creativity in my design work, and started serving others with it instead.

The question I used to I ask myself:

"Is this cool, mysterious, and beautiful enough?"

The question I now I ask myself:

"Is this clear, usable, and accessible enough?"

What people think is that the 2nd question means I don't care about creativity, imagination, and beauty anymore.

But I'm sorry peeps, those people are wrong.

Creativity loves a good brief: and accessibility is the best brief of all.

To say it with [Karl Groves](https://www.linkedin.com/in/karlgroves/):

"If you think accessibility necessitates sacrificing the visual appeal of your product, then you have a creativity problem, not an accessibility problem.

Edit: or maybe both."

Hard agree on this one.

Plus: who ever said that creativity is only about the visuals? There is SO much more to it than that.

Want to find out more on how accessibility can be creative?

Join us at [Hatch Conference](https://www.linkedin.com/company/hatch-conference/) in September\!

[https://lnkd.in/dtbstA4P](https://lnkd.in/dtbstA4P)

Or if you can't do that, watch me debunk the myth on stage at [UX Copenhagen](https://www.linkedin.com/company/uxcopenhagen/): [https://lnkd.in/dZfGTaHh](https://lnkd.in/dZfGTaHh)

Or DM me for a chat – always happy to talk\!

### **Friday 4 April 2025 – compliance**

[https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri\_is-compliance-the-only-thing-that-matters-activity-7313937218375491584-XVi5?utm\_source=share\&utm\_medium=member\_desktop\&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri_is-compliance-the-only-thing-that-matters-activity-7313937218375491584-XVi5?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU)

Is "compliance" the only thing that matters to you?

When I work with organisations to train their staff and empower them to reach digital accessibility, they often focus on achieving a specific WCAG conformance level: typically Level AA.

This is a practical baseline: I understand that it makes sense. But focusing solely on compliance risks missing easy opportunities to improve accessibility in ways that benefit all users; and ultimately, the business.

Take Level AAA. Often seen as unattainable across an entire website – but you know what? Some criteria within this level are easy to implement, and they can make a huge difference.

As proof of this, see my post from the other day: on acronyms, and how these useful shortcuts can actually create barriers (https://bit.ly/43KB0Vb). I mentioned speakers of English as a foreign language as an affected category. Interestingly though, all those who commented agreeing with me were native speakers – which kind of proves my point.

You also might have noticed I didn’t mention any official WCAG criteria to justify my request for clarity. That’s because I honestly never think about compliance first.

Also because if I had, you’d have found that while a criterion on acronyms does exist, your company may not be interested in adopting it – because it’s Level AAA.

Why is it Level AAA, when it’s so easy to do and most people would appreciate it? I have no idea.

That criterion is in good company. Here are a few Level AAA WCAG criteria that should really be A – and that your organisation should implement anyway:

SC 3.1.4: Abbreviations 

Provide a mechanism to identify the expanded form of acronyms. Add tooltips – or simply explain them in the text. It’s basic good writing (and good manners).

SC 3.1.3: Unusual Words 

Same as above. Explaining jargon helps those with cognitive disabilities or anyone unfamiliar with the terminology.

SC 1.4.8: Visual Presentation 

As a designer who started in editorial design, I can’t get over how this is AAA. It’s about readability: line length, line spacing, justified text. These have a huge impact on people with dyslexia, cognitive disabilities, low vision, or just an allergy to bad design. Basic good design rules, easy to achieve.

These aren’t the only AAA criteria that are oddly listed as such: just the ones I think can make the biggest difference with minimal effort.

So please: don’t focus solely on compliance. Instead: adopt an accessibility-first mindset. 

Look for improvements that are easy to implement and meaningful to users, regardless of their WCAG level. With the added benefit of improving compliance anyway.

And if you need support: I’m here. You or your company can hire me to audit, train, educate – and get ready for the European Accessibility Act.

Why me? Because I have a unique take, shaped by my design background. I focus on the bigger picture: empathy, UX, creativity, branding – and the business’s need to make money.

You know what to do – my DMs are open.

### **Mon 7 April – empty list**

[https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri\_accessibility-a11y-accessibilitymatters-activity-7314957980033032192-EWgE?utm\_source=share\&utm\_medium=member\_desktop\&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri_accessibility-a11y-accessibilitymatters-activity-7314957980033032192-EWgE?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU)

Here’s a list of accessibility issues that I’m not affected by, because I don’t identify as disabled:

1. …  
2. …  
3. …  
4. …  
5. …  
6. …

….

Yes that’s right: it’s an empty list.

Because the actual percentage of (potentially) disabled people in the world is 100%. It definitely includes me – with the recent memory of my right arm immobilised in a sling still vividly fresh: navigating life, and the web, is way harder when you're down a limb.

It definitely includes you. And also all of your customers and site visitors, bar none.

After all, as Cindi Li was probably the first to say, we’re only ever temporarily abled.

Have you ever broken a limb? Or had conjunctivitis? Or a blocked ear? Or a child in your arms? Or the sun in your eyes?

“Over 1 billion people around the world live with some form of disability. It is estimated that one in five people have a permanent disability, but 100% of the population will be faced with vision, hearing, motion, or cognitive disabilities at some point in their lives.” 

(by @Sara Soueidan).

Yes. A hundred per cent includes me, and it also includes you.

So when you next think “What’s in it for me?” before you invest in accessibility, here’s your answer: you’re investing in a possible future you.

Design for your future self. And for the present selves of billions of people, who need you to be thinking about them, too.

And for this reason, accessibility is one of the best marketing tools available to us: check the comments for a document I compiled, providing you with abundant proof.

But if that's not enough, and you want to hear it from me directly: send me a message, and I'll explain how you can turn accessibility into an opportunity, instead of a perceived limitation.

Image description: the last slide in my talk “Dizzy users close browsers”, with the famous quote from Sir Tim Berners-Lee, the father of the World Wide Web: “The power of the Web is in its universality. Access by everyone regardless of disability  is an essential aspect”.

You can watch the talk on the UX Copenhagen YouTube channel, or on the Beyond Tellerrand Berlin 2023 page.

\#accessibility \#a11y \#AccessibilityMatters \#DontBeADick

### **Wed 9 April – No looking back**

[https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri\_no-looking-back-once-you-get-started-you-activity-7315670241022222338-G4qY?utm\_source=share\&utm\_medium=member\_desktop\&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri_no-looking-back-once-you-get-started-you-activity-7315670241022222338-G4qY?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU)

No looking back.

Once you get started, you can't stop.

A few years ago \[be honest Piccia: it was way more than a few\], I made the shift from being a creative designer to focusing fully on UX.

I threw myself into the world of user journeys, personas, microcopy, and everything else that makes a digital experience feel seamless – increasing conversions as the cherry on the cake.

I was all in. Obsessed with delivering the best experience possible.

And then I came across accessibility.

And it very soon dawned on me: there is no such thing as good UX if a portion of your audience – whatever the size – is excluded by design.

 

If someone can’t read the text, can’t use a keyboard to navigate, can't know the content of an essential image for lack of alt text, or is stuck waiting for a screen reader to plough through ten pointless links before getting to the main content – then the experience is not only bad, it’s broken. For everyone, regardless of ability.

 

That realisation changed everything. It changed the way I design, the way I think, and the way I teach.

 

So now, accessibility isn’t an add-on.

 

It is the user experience.

 

And once you start seeing it like that, there is no looking back.

That's why everything I do now, is from an accessibility-first point of view.

There can be no looking back.

### **Fri 11 April 2025  Wired to be selfish**

[https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:share:7316499962219884544/](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:share:7316499962219884544/)

We are wired to be selfish.

We only really care if something affects us directly.

 

Would I rail against irresponsible website animations making people sick, if I didn't have a vestibular disorder?

 

Would I care about colour contrast making some websites invisible, if I had the same eyesight as 20 years ago?

 

Would I campaign for left-aligned text, if centred text blocks didn't look like flying saucers or flower vases to me?

 

As I get my accessible data visualisations workshop ready to be delivered next week, I ask myself whether my campaign for accessibility isn't just self-serving, simply aimed at easing my own discomfort and improving my own life.

 

That's why I always do my best to make sure I also advocate for issues that go beyond my own lived experience.

 

Minorities need allies.

People disabled by society need allies.

Humanity needs allies.

 

People are good: we need to educate them. Make sure that those who didn't know, now know.

Good karma beats bad karma – every time.

 

In the meantime: it's Friday, again\!

I'm going to work on an exciting side hustle all weekend.

What about you?

Wishing you all that you can possibly desire out of the next two days.

p.s. the  Accessible Data Visualisations Masterclass is next Wednesday 16 April, and it's going to be way more fun than the title promises. No fancy sales page, but I promise, it's a class of substance, delivering way more than anyone may think – you will be glad you enrolled. And if it's not for you: maybe it's for a friend? Link in comments.

Image description: A street with roadworks on the left of the image, while on the right on a black metal surface are two stencils: from the left, the words "good karma" stacked vertically with a bicycle in between them, in bright yellow; to the right of this, the words "bad karma" stacked vertically with a car in between them, in bright orange. Meaning: bikes are good karma, cars are bad karma.

### **Tue 15 April 2025 – Confession time**

[https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:share:7317920590152007683/](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:share:7317920590152007683/)

Confession time.

I used to be a design snob. Especially when it came to tools.

Yes, Canva, I'm looking at you.

When it launched, Canva made browser-based design stupidly easy (and occasionally stupidly frustrating, but let’s not digress). Suddenly, anyone with a mouse and a pulse could whip up a flyer, a book cover, or a long-form PDF.

Cue my designer ego having a full-blown hissy fit.

But credit where credit’s due: Canva kept growing, and improving. It’s now a benevolent giant, genuinely impressive in what it can do.

Did you catch the latest keynote? Somewhere between vaudeville and kumbaya, it quietly announced something game-changing: soon, users will be able to build and launch fully formed websites in Canva.

Will this revolutionise enterprise workflows?

Maybe not.

Will marketing teams love spinning up a microsite without begging devs for help?

Probably.

Am I going to drop the snobbery and try it when it launches?

Absolutely.

But here’s the catch: for all its power and reach, Canva still has a massive blind spot.

Accessibility.

Their tool isn’t accessible. Their marketing site isn’t either. And with the European Accessibility Act now enforceable, they could be open to lawsuits.

Will it come to that? Who knows. But what if they'd built accessibility in from the start?

So here’s my message:

Be like Canva. But also… don’t be like Canva.

Build accessibility in from the start – whether there’s a lawsuit looming or not. For so many reasons\! Not last: let's not be dicks to disabled people – after all, we are only ever temporarily abled.

And while we’re on the subject: my workshop on accessible data visualisations is tomorrow Wednesday, 16 April at 4pm CET.

It’s not just about charts. We’ll cover words, colour, alternatives to colour, and how to use alt text properly — for infographics 𝘢𝘯𝘥 everything else.

As a little thank you for the WordPress crowd (I was on [Nathan Wrigley](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/#)'s show WP Builds yesterday), here’s a last-minute €10 discount when you use the code: WPBUILDS10 at checkout.

Link in comments because LinkedIn gets irate when we promote our own links on this internet website\! Even though it has "link" in its own name\!

[\#accessibility](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/#) [\#a11y](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/#) [\#DontBeADick](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/#)

### **Friday 25 April 2025 – accessiBe fine (recycled from January)**

[https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri\_a11y-accessibiliity-dontbeadick-activity-7321482819120037890-ZVbr?utm\_source=share\&utm\_medium=member\_desktop\&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri_a11y-accessibiliity-dontbeadick-activity-7321482819120037890-ZVbr?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU)

It's confirmed\!

Dishonesty is definitely not a good business growth strategy.

The FTC (the US Federal Trade Commission) has approved a final consent order against the accessibility overlay company [accessiBe](https://www.linkedin.com/company/accessibe/) to the tune of 1 million USD, to potentially pay off clients misled by deceptive claims misrepresenting the capabilities of its product to make websites accessible.

Please forgive me while I rejoice.

No, I'm not gloating at someone else's misfortune: I'm celebrating the hopeful beginning of an easier digital life for disabled users.

The problem with overlays basically is that they exist.

An overlay is automated software that claims to resolve accessibility issues by adding a widget on your website, giving users, among other things, the ability to tweak the interface to their preference.

What\! I can hear you say\! Is this magic? How cool\! Just by adding a widget, I can sort out my accessibility issues\!

It sounds too good to be true, doesn't it?

Well, that's because it is.

Clients and designers who want to do right by disabled people, among them some of you,  often ask me about using an overlay tool.

It's completely understandable that you would ask that: most overlay companies look completely legit, their claims bold and believable, their interfaces smooth and easy to use...

...unless you're a disabled user, already using assistive technology.

In this crucial use case, overlays often interfere with the experience, sometimes preventing access altogether.

But the worst thing about overlay companies is that they claim the absolute opposite, presenting their tool as an all-in-one solution to all your accessibility woes. And because these overlays companies often have big money backing (accessiBe is definitely one of those), their marketing departments do an excellent job of persuading non-experts that their product will make your sites accessible.

It won't. Don't fall for it.

Besides often creating a technical obstacle, here are a few of the crucial aspects of accessibility that an overlay won't cover:

– Missing headings or wrong HTML structure

– Missing alt text on images (AI is still crap at this, not lastly because it mostly ignores context)

– Wrong link text

– Missing labels on form fields

– Placeholder text instead of labels on forms

– No submit button on form or no clear submit button label

I'll stop not to bore you, but you've guessed, there would be more.

So, next time a client or boss asks about using an overlay, you know what to do: just say no. There are much better ways of not being a dick to disabled people.

In case you need back-up info on why a resounding "NO" is the only possible answer, please find a few resources in the comments, including the FTC article on the fine, and a reminder that accessibility is an opportunity, not a limitation.

Want help on accessibility? DM, or let me know in the comments 👇🏼

Find this useful/ interesting? Give it a share 💙

[hashtag\#a11y](https://www.linkedin.com/search/results/all/?keywords=%23a11y&origin=HASH_TAG_FROM_FEED) [hashtag\#accessibiliity](https://www.linkedin.com/search/results/all/?keywords=%23accessibiliity&origin=HASH_TAG_FROM_FEED) [hashtag\#DontBeADick](https://www.linkedin.com/search/results/all/?keywords=%23dontbeadick&origin=HASH_TAG_FROM_FEED)

### **Sun 27 April – Sundays are for painting**

[https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:share:7322160017065803776/](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:share:7322160017065803776/)

Sundays are for painting.

In fact, every single day should be for painting.

One day, ALL my days will be for painting\!

What are your Sundays for – that you wish was for every single day?

Image description: Watercolour painting: alternating stripes of deep red, mauve, sepia, a thin strip of bright fluo pink, and more red, heavily textured and also a bit splattered, with colours interaction. Like the phases of an old building, painted over many times. A red opaque square painted in acrylic sits in the middle, on top of the stripes. On handmade cotton rag paper by Khadi.

### **Tue 29 April – Apagón**

[https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:share:7322935050348445697/](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:share:7322935050348445697/)

Things I learnt during a nationwide power and communications outage:

– Never watch conspiracy theory action movies ever again (I don't as a rule, but even so, they were all replaying in my head).

– Always have an old-style phone, even better if rotary, plugged into your phone line (it may just work\!).

– Re-build a record and video collection that does not rely on the internet to be viewed or listened to (the power came back, 7 hours later, but not the connection).

– Consider a career that does not rely on either power or comms (are there any left? Even bakeries had to close)

– Buy an analogue radio and a reserve of fresh batteries.

– Better still, build a radio station so you can transmit as well as receive.

– Have some cash in the house.

– Think that there are places in the world where this is a daily occurrence. Be aware of your luck.

– Make sure that your last message to your far-away family of origin group chat, before the lines cut off for 12 hours, isn't: "I am actually terrified".

What would you think if power and comms went off in the entirety of the country you live in?

### **Tue 6 May 2025 – Bad news first**

[https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:share:7325488103970754560/](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:share:7325488103970754560/)

Bad news first:

WCAG compliance does not mean actual accessibility.

Huge bummer innit\!

\[WCAG is the acronym for Web Accessibility Compliance Guidelines\]

Here's a few reasons why:

– WCAG do not consider \*all\* accessibility issues.

– WCAG do not consider all best practices. 

– WCAG do not consider overall inclusion.

– WCAG do not consider usability.

And here are a few examples of bad practices that cause accessibility issues and that are ignored by the WCAG:

– Use of low-contrast typefaces

– Use of complicated layouts

– Use of unfamiliar patterns

– Use of more than one H1

– Use of too many emojis

– Use of autoplay video\*

\*(no, it’s not enough to provide a stop button, or to make it stop after 5 seconds as the WCAG recommend. I only need 2-3 seconds to get sick. Please no autoplay – and no GIFs in emails, I can't stop them and won't be able to read your missive)

My point being: there is a big difference between providing a technically and legally compliant service, and providing a service that is truly accessible and inclusive.

Stop asking the question “does it comply with WCAG?” 

Start asking the question “can everybody use this?”

OK, that was the bad news.

And here's the brilliant news\!

Adopting inclusion will expand your business (and, why not, your karma too). And not by a little: by a hell of a lot.

Don't believe me? I've got proof\!

I've compiled a doc with recent research and data making the business case for accessibility, and demonstrating how accessibility is an unbelievably sound, long-term business strategy and growth driver – in bags of money as well as good karma.

Go get your copy here:

[https://piccianeri.com/business-case-accessibility/](https://piccianeri.com/business-case-accessibility/)

It may well be what you need to finally persuade your marketing department, or that of your clients, that they should embrace accessibility for better reasons than fear of legal trouble.

And here's Ciro the orange cat purely for the algo, and your delectation. He's lying on a bed on his back, with front paws up in delight as he's receiving the belly rubs he so dearly loves.

[\#a11y](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/?shareActive=true&view=management#) [\#DontBeADick](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/?shareActive=true&view=management#) [\#GoodKarma](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/?shareActive=true&view=management#) [\#Accessibility](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/?shareActive=true&view=management#)

### **8 May – save money, train your staff**

Want to save money – and make money – on accessibility?

Train your staff.

I say this to every client, and I’ll keep saying it because it’s true: if you invest in accessibility training for your team now, you’ll spend a lot less fixing problems later.

And you will also open up your audience to a lot more people.

Here’s what happens when your team doesn’t know accessibility:

– They create inaccessible content

– You have to rely on expensive external help

– You waste time retro-fitting instead of doing it right the first time

– You risk legal trouble (trust me on this one – the European Accessibility Act will open up the floor to this)

– You shut out 25% of your audience – yes, that’s the percentage of people who have a disability at any given time

Here’s what happens when your team does know accessibility:

– They create accessible content from the start

– You don’t have to call in expensive external help as often

– You reach a bigger audience and make more money

– You avoid legal headaches

– You build a better, more inclusive brand

– You turn accessibility into an opportunity – instead of a limitation

The choice is yours. But if you’re going to wait until you get sued, lose customers, or have to rebuild everything from scratch… don’t say I didn’t warn you.

2 things you can do now:

– Download my handy guide to the European Accessibility Act, and check whether it applies to you or not: got to piccianeri dot com/eaa/ (proper link in comments)

– Send me a message to chat about how I can help you turn accessibility into an opportunity, by training your teams.

The time is most definitely now. Just do it\!

And here's Tsatsiki the fluffy white cat curled up in a basket for the algo, and for your delectation. He's seen from above and is looking up at the camera with huge blue eyes.
