There's a hint of orange in the light markings on his face, and on his coat. It's clear from the floof volume that he'd smell of talcum powder, should you bury your face in that gorgeous softness. Much missed – we adored you Ziki, and you adored us back. Thank you.

### **12 May – Figma sites**

Start as you mean to go on.

If this tenet is true, I don't hold much hope for the future of Figma sites.

Unless you’ve been under a rock, or you have nothing to do with the design world, you’ve probably heard that Figma’s Config conference has just taken place, and CEO and co-founder Dylan Field announced a whole bunch of new features.

Many people have been swooning all over the announcement – sadly, I wasn't one of them.

I don’t have the time today to explain to you why it doesn’t make me happy to see Figma go the Adobe way (i.e.: hyper-bloated, gigantic Jack of all trades who lost sight of their base more than a decade ago).

But I am indeed making sure I do have the time today to warn you that you should not publish your sites with Figma Sites...

...Unless what you really want is to create barriers for your users and churn out a div soup to rival that produced by the worst page builders.

In short: the new Figma Sites web publishing platform is basically a “horrific div generator”.

But Piccia, I can hear you say. It’s just a beta version\! It will improve with time\!

Hmmm… Here's why I don't hold much hope that it will:

The code generated by Figma Sites completely bypasses semantic HTML, even for basic elements such as headings.

Why oh why I am asking you, why would you deliberately choose to deploy a div with a class to output an H1, when you already have a beautifully simple semantic HTML tag for the job?

A no-code platform generates code anyway.

And if it starts by ignoring the building blocks of the web – the same ones that make a product robust – I’m sorry, but I simply don’t trust it to sort that out in the future, when it’s precisely what it should have started from.

You’ve heard it from me a million times and you’ll hear it from me once more:

It is way cheaper to start the right way, than remediate afterwards.

Figma Sites appears to have been conceived with no regard for best practices, and at the moment it produces websites that are an embarrassing accessibility fail.

A few very good resources that explain how and why this is happening:

– Do Not Publish Your Designs on the Web with Figma Sites… by Adrian Roselli. Constantly being updated, with a list of other resources.

https://adrianroselli.com/2025/05/do-not-publish-your-designs-on-the-web-with-figma-sites.html

– A post by Emily Lawes with a mini- accessibility review. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/emilylawes\_accessibility-figma-a11y-activity-7326207820545028096-0RUN

– Figma’s New Horrific Div Generator by Anthony Alicea. https://youtu.be/dS8E5FlyId8?si=kTlUTDRkTUXYf\_xe

– And if all of the above is not enough: a video by Kevin Powell sporting a big thumb facing down on the cover. https://youtu.be/ZsFIvULxkHI?si=TgqOd3gnemmnKs4n

It gives me no joy to shoot this new tool down.

But every time a div is used instead of the correct semantic HTML element, a virtual kitten dies.

Follow me, or send me a message, to find out how to make virtual kittens thrive.

### **Wed 14 May – Page Builder Summit**

This Friday I am talking at the Page Builder Summit\!

I will present a talk with the title "Data Visualisations for Everyone".

It is completely free to watch, so if you missed out on the accessible data visualisation masterclass I gave last month, but you are interested in the subject, do make sure you sign up to the summit and watch my talk.

https://pagebuildersummit.com/?affiliate=piccia

And even if you aren't particularly interested in my talk, there are many interesting presentations that have nothing to do with page builders and that you should definitely watch.   

In honour of the summit,  I'm giving away the recordings of the Accessible Data Visualisations masterclass with a 30% discount. Link and code in the comments\!

So many great people are presenting at the Summit, here are just a few of my favourites:

Nicole Osborne ⭐️ cracking open the secrets on how to win on LinkedIn

Courtney Robertson on leveraging AI tools to better run your digital agency

Akshat Choudhary on crimes committed by page builders who kill performance

Sarah Guilliot on how to use storytelling for success

Tammie Lister on the Lego principle for web builders – this one you surely cannot miss – it has Lego in it

and many many more.

Hope you'll sign up\! What are you waiting for? It's free\!\!

### **15 May – GAAD**

Today is the third Thursday in May, which means it's Global Accessibility Awareness Day (GAAD).

"The purpose of GAAD is to get everyone talking, thinking and learning about digital access and inclusion, and the more than One Billion people with disabilities/impairments."

There are loads of events all over the world, both in person and virtual, to mark the day and increase awareness of accessibility.

The GAAD website has a page where many events are listed.

https://accessibility.day/

I have a busy day teaching an in-person class (yes\! actual people\! flesh and blood, meat-space students\!), but I'm making sure I'll keep space for at least one webinar:

How to convince people to invest in accessibility

Led by Stéphanie Walter.

https://resources.testingtime.com/how-to-convince-people-to-invest-in-accessibility

I know, because you've told me, that many of you have this problem, and I also find myself having to push for the cause with clients. I'm looking forward to gaining new insights and ammunition from Stéphanie, which I'll make sure I'll add to my free download The business case for accessibility.

https://piccianeri.com/business-case-accessibility/

Have a happy GAAD and may you be granted access wherever you wish or need to\!

### **Monday 19 May – EAA bite**

You know one thing that I hear way too often?

“The design and marketing staff are keen to learn and implement accessibility, but management aren’t sold on it yet”

I usually say that I'd rather lead with the positive than use fear-mongering tactics, but...

Seriously?

What is it going to take for management to work it out?

You've heard me many times before and here I go again, like a broken record, reminding you if you are the boss, and reminding you to remind your bosses if you’re not the boss, that the European Accessibility Act is about to come and bite you in the arse – 40 days from today.

(Although in fact, in most EU countries the legislation is already in force).

These are 3 simple questions you need to answer, in order to find out whether the EAA applies to you – or your clients:

• Does the company sell online in the EU?

• Is the company’s annual revenue over 2 million euros?

• Does the company have more than 10 employees?

If you’ve answered “yes” to all 3 of the questions above, but the company doesn’t have an accessibility program yet: we need to talk. (And management doesn’t really have an option any longer).

Should you need a reminder, as well as ammunition with your C-Suite, find the link in the comments to  my handy guide to the EAA. You can check there whether your company falls under any of the excluded categories.

If it doesn’t: I can help.

 Comment here or send a DM: I can empower you to make sure the EAA – and accessibility – are an opportunity, and not a limitation, or even, let's be honest, a massive, biting pain in the arse.

Image description: The open mutt of a very scary dog with fangs out and red eyes, ready to bite, jumping at you so fast that its ears are blurry.

### **Thursday 22 May – re-intro**

Hey LinkedIn\! 

It’s been ages since I last introduced myself, and I got many new contacts recently: here’s my story in a post.

Nationality & education:

Born and raised in Tuscany, Italy.

BA from the University of Siena & an MA from the Courtauld Institute in London, both in Art History.

Countless design, UX, accessibility courses.

Early 90s:

Post MA: returned to Tuscany, with few job prospects.

So, my friends and I invented our ideal job: an arts publishing company. We edited, designed & published art catalogues & books for cultural institutions.

I designed my first book with QuarkXPress on the tiny, b/w screen of a Mac Classic (same number of pixels as the tiniest icon on a smartphone).

1998 onwards:

Moved to London in Cool Britannia times – ah, the vibe. Designed books for institutions such as the National Gallery, Tate Gallery, British Museum, British Council, etc, and  art galleries. Freelanced for design agencies (branding and corporate design) and for the Sunday Times Magazine. Became a full-on visual creative and image-maker (using photography and illustration).

For a few years I directed the design at the British Film Institute (BFI), which gave me the joy of watching films for a living and of full-on visual creativity, as well as lifelong friendships. That brought on collaborations with many film festivals, such as the Palestine Film Festival.

2012 onwards: 

Dived deeper into web design and UX, which I had started at the BFI. Learnt the basics of coding (HTML, CSS & a sprinkle of PHP): I recommend this to all designers. Started attending the London WordPress meetups, and designing WordPress sites.

2013 onwards:

The following years were super hard. Early menopause; traumatising changes for my family of origin in Italy; a devastating new government in the UK; the long illness and death of one parent, and the steep decline of the other; a general feeling of being adrift, no future.

2016 onwards:

So in 2016 I did what any irresponsible person would do: I abandoned the rich tapestry of my cosy life in London, where I had solid, loyal friends and a myriad contacts, and moved to Valencia, a new city in a new country where I didn’t know anyone and had no job.

Since then, I firmly positioned myself as a UX/ UI hybrid, which is  a thing (fight me).

But most importantly – I embraced accessibility: my current mission, and one I don't believe I'll ever abandon.

I'm an accessible design lead, consultant, educator, and speaker. (Next in-person conference appearance: Hatch Conference in Berlin.)

I use my creative background and skills to deliver the most accessible design and UX possible, to the wider possible audience.

What about you?

Tell me please – I'd love to know.

### **Fri 23 May – Data viz**

Last year, something completely unexpected happened to me. 

I started working with an NGO providing a range of services related to the child welfare system in the US – which in itself isn't that surprising, it was a referral from a friend. 

Within such a line of work, this NGO has to rely on data visualisations rather heavily. Given the sensitive nature of their services, they are also – quite rightly – concerned about making all of their content as inclusive and as accessible as possible. 

Inclusion is a wider spectrum than accessibility, by the way: accessibility relates more closely to disabled people, while inclusion aims at universal reach and diversity. 

Working with this NGO meant that I had to delve deep into the data viz aspect of the content, helping them make their charts accessible, as well as understandable.

 

And that's when something happened – that I never would have predicted:

I totally nerded out on accessible data viz.

I, a recovering creative who wouldn't touch numerical data with a bargepole, because I usually can't make head or tail of it – got fully sucked into the magical world of the stories that statistics and data can tell, and into how we can make sure that everyone can enjoy them, regardless of abilities.

The biggest surprise of all?

I am actually good at data visualisation – and have great fun with it, too.

Who would have thought\! Knock me down with a feather. 

This led to a few nice things:

• more work with the lovely NGO

• a workshop on accessible data visualisations

• participation in the open source project Visua11y

• that won the overall prize at the CloudFest Hackathon 2025

• a talk on accessible data visualisations at the Page Builder Summit.  

And that's the point of this post: I've just published the video of the talk with full transcript on my website. And I'm bravely posting the link here, even though I know that LI will punish me for it.

 

Data Visualisations for Everyone

https://piccianeri.com/accessible-data-visualisations-talk/

 

Should the talk get you as excited about data viz as I am – you never know – you can use the coupon code PBS30w to get a 30% discount on Get It Seen: Accessible Data Visualisations, namely the recordings and all materials of the workshop on the topic I recently delivered. And here goes another link\!

https://piccianeri.com/enroll-accessible-data-visualisations/ 

However you really don't have to buy anything at all in order to learn quite a bit on data visualisations and how to make them accessible, simply by watching the video and reading the transcript of the Data Visualisations for Everyone talk.


Thanks as ever to my friends Anchen 🌟 le Roux 🌟 and Nathan Wrigley for the opportunity to speak at their summit.

### **Monday 26 May 2025 – head in sand** 

Did you know that the myth that ostriches bury their heads in the sand when they're in danger, and plunge into a state of denial, is actually a misconception?

They are, I am told, much cleverer than that.

But it looks like us humans have taken that legend and made it our own.

 

I've finally transcribed the @Hatch Conference talk with the rather blunt title The European Accessibility Act is Coming to Bite You in the Arse, and I published both video and transcript on my website.

Even though I've talked on here about the EAA at length, and mentioned it many times – I'd never actually looked at the text of the talk again (or goddess forbid, watched the video 😱).

 After editing the transcript, and almost 9 months after the conference, I confess:

I am in shock at how many companies are still in total denial about the looming deadline. 

Just 34 days to go until the EAA goes into full force on 28 June 2025 (although legislation is already active in all EU member states).

Should you panic?

No. Panic is never a good policy.

But you should definitely take action, if you haven't yet.

 It's never too late – no time like the present\!

Things you can do right now: 

1. Watch the EAA talk if you haven't already (speed it up if time is of the essence – link in comments)  
2. Skim through the transcript and extract the most biting truth bombs to show your bosses  
3. Send those truth bombs, together with the Guide to the European Accessibility Act, to your decision makers, in a strongly worded email  
4. Include also a few stats from The Business Case for Accessibility, to sweeten the pill and increase the chances of success  
5. Get in touch with me to talk about your best course of action in the given time – likely to be along these lines: Get your site/ app audited Get your staff trained\! Empower them to produce accessible content from now on Cherry on the cake: see your site's performance and profitability soar.  
6. Comment, like, share this post with as many people as possible, so they get their own head, or their bosses' head, out of the sand, now.

Whether working with me or someone else: do something.

 

And remember: accessibility is not a limitation. It's a great opportunity.

I will keep reminding you of this fact, until I'm blue in the face.

And I'm ready to present actual facts and figures that prove this, to anyone who disagrees (but doesn't bring any evidence).

Go out and grab that opportunity\!

Let's make the web a better place for everyone.

Image description: The black silhouette of an ostrich standing against the sunset, bending with its long neck all the way into the ground, the head invisible. It's probably digging a safer space for its eggs – not in denial from danger.

![][image1]

### **Tue 23 Sept 2025** 

[https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri\_do-you-have-a-side-hustle-if-you-don-activity-7376269046595457024-qhgi?utm\_source=share\&utm\_medium=member\_desktop\&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri_do-you-have-a-side-hustle-if-you-don-activity-7376269046595457024-qhgi?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU)

\[months of neglect\! I have to update\!)

Do you have a side hustle?

If you don’t, and you work in tech: I warmly suggest you start an “inexpensive experiment” (© @Debbie Levitt) soon – but that’s another post.

I have a side hustle I recently started “planting seeds” for, that will end up in a guide to Valencia, where I live. As a consequence, I’m in the process of visiting all the attractions in the city. It could get worse, as far as side hustles go – also considering it involves quite a bit of sampling local delicacies.

Because of what I do, I started asking a number of questions wherever I go. Such as:

– Is this place accessible on a wheelchair or a mobility scooter?

– How close can you get by car?

– Is there an audio guide?

– Is it descriptive?

– Is there a written alternative to the audio guide?

– Braille captions?

– Large text captions?

Then when you look at the exhibits, these are a few of the things that tend to happen:

– Captions on the walls miles away from the actual exhibit.

– Hard to get close to, at an awkward height.

– Tiny typeface in a dimly lit room.

– Often assuming specific knowledge.

Here's my light bulb moment, at long last:

What if my guide included accessibility details? Real-world information making it easier to enjoy a place, regardless of ability.

Sections like:

– Getting There (parking distances, step-free routes)

– What You'll Find (lighting conditions, text sizes, audio options)

– Making the Most of It (best times to visit, etc)

As I'm already exploring every corner of this beautiful, multi-layered city, it would be silly not to document it in a way that helps more people get out there and explore it too.

Honestly kind of obvious innit\! Lucky I thought about it\!

What staggers me is that, as far as I know, your average guide usually doesn’t include any kind of information on the accessibility of a tourist attraction.

Can you imagine having to call in advance every single time? How much longer would it take you to organise the simplest trip?

Adding those pieces of info will only expand the guide and make it more useful for everyone. Not only disabled people. Just like what happens with digital accessibility.

All it takes is a change of perspective: basic human empathy applied in a conscious way. Start putting yourself in other people’s shoes, and you notice that ramp is too steep, those captions are unreadable, that audio guide is useless when you are deaf.

That’s exactly the mindset shift I teach in my Accessible Design and Content course: the course I wish had existed when I started learning about accessibility – coming to your screens very soon. Stay tuned for updates.

Oh and if you’re planning to visit Valencia, let me know and I’ll pass on the Insta account details.

Image description: The neo-classic/ neo-baroque facade of the Ayuntamiento (Town Hall) in Valencia, against a bright blue sky and with tree branches above it.
